text:
*[Bug 19086] New: nlminb() no longer works reliably with bounds*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19086>

            Bug ID: 19086
           Summary: nlminb() no longer works reliably with bounds
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.6.x
          Hardware: All
                OS: All
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Low-level
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:kaskr@dtu.dk|kaskr@dtu.dk>

Created attachment 3729
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3729&action=edit>
Patch against R-devel

This is a follow-up on a long standing nlminb() bug intitially reported here:
<https://hypatia.math.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2019-January/077211.html>
Martin Maechler provided a summary of affected platforms here:
<https://hypatia.math.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2019-February/077257.html>

A short recap: After the release of R-3.5.0, I noticed that nlminb() was
suddenly failing for a lot of optimization problems that used to work. Common
for them all was that they used bounds. I boiled down a simple example
demonstrating the problem:

f <- function(x) sum( log(diff(x)^2+.01) + (x[1]-1)^2 )
opt <- nlminb(rep(0, 10), f, lower=-1, upper=3)
xhat <- rep(1, 10)
abs( opt$objective - f(xhat) ) < 1e-4  ## Must be TRUE

Notably, the example converges in ~68 iterations when removing the bounds.
When supplying bounds the example fails to converge within the default 150
iterations. That holds even when increasing the bounds to a level where the
problem is effectively unconstrained.

Solution: In portsrc.f (lines 9620-9621) two matrix vector products are carried
out, essentially corresponding to (x=t(L)%*%x; x=L%*%x):
    CALL DL7TVM(N, V(X01), V(L), V(X01))
    CALL DL7VML(N, V(X01), V(L), V(X01))
These calls are passing identical 2nd and 4th arguments, which violate the
Fortran aliasing rules! When compiled with optimization, the resulting x
becomes a zero vector regardless of its value on input.
To fix the argument aliasing issue one can simply substitute a temporary like
this:
    CALL DL7TVM(N, V(TEMP1), V(L), V(X01))
    CALL DL7VML(N, V(X01), V(L), V(TEMP1))

See attached 'portsrc.patch'. With this simple patch nlminb() with bounds works
again!

Martin Maechler provided a summary of affected platforms here:

%x):

    CALL DL7VML(N, V(X01), V(L), V(X01))
These calls are passing identical 2nd and 4th arguments, which violate the

text:
*[Bug 19069] aperm(X, 1:length(dim(X))) should short-circuit and just return X.*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19069>

--- Comment #14 from Kurt Hornik (<mailto:Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at|Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at>) ---
So here we go again ...
I now committed 

r90136 | hornik | 2026-06-12 09:14:40 +0200 (Fri, 12 Jun 2026) | 1 line
Have aperm.default() copy attributes as documented since 2006.

and 

r90137 | hornik | 2026-06-12 10:53:54 +0200 (Fri, 12 Jun 2026) | 1 line
Short-circuit identity permutation (PR#19069), second try.

with the latter using a modified version of the suggested patch (only 
when resize is true).

All CRAN packages which had failed with c90023 worked fine for me with the
above changes, so hopefully we can close the issue now.

elements.text: [Bug 19069] aperm(X, 1:length(dim(X))) should short-circuit and just return X.

) ---
So here we go again ...
I now committed 

r90136 | hornik | 2026-06-12 09:14:40 +0200 (Fri, 12 Jun 2026) | 1 line
Have aperm.default() copy attributes as documented since 2006.

and 

r90137 | hornik | 2026-06-12 10:53:54 +0200 (Fri, 12 Jun 2026) | 1 line
Short-circuit identity permutation (PR#19069), second try.

with the latter using a modified version of the suggested patch (only 
when resize is true).

All CRAN packages which had failed with c90023 worked fine for me with the
above changes, so hopefully we can close the issue now.

text:
*[Bug 19087] New: Security issue with `pdf()`/`postscript()`*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19087>

            Bug ID: 19087
           Summary: Security issue with `pdf()`/`postscript()`
           Product: R
           Version: R-devel (trunk)
          Hardware: Other
                OS: Other
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: I/O
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:h.wickham@gmail.com|h.wickham@gmail.com>

`file` is, in the vast majority of cases, a path — inert data naming where
bytes should land. But in `pdf()` and `postscript()`, when its first character
is `|`, the remainder is handed to `R_popen()`, i.e. executed by `/bin/sh -c`.
So `pdf(file = x)` is a write-to-disk operation for almost all values of `x`
and an arbitrary-command-execution operation for a small, invisible subset of
them. Nothing at the call site signals that the second mode exists, and it
requires a close read of the documentation to discover it exists.

It's reasonable to expect that a careful R programmer will know that `system()`
is potentially dangerous when given unsanitized user inputs; I don't think they
have any particular reason to suspect that `pdf()` is just as bad. This matters
because filenames are routinely derived from untrusted input. R is now deployed
in Shiny, plumber, OpenCPU and batch report-generation services where it's
reasonable for the filename to be a user-specified parameter.

The cost of removing this functionality is very small because the legitimate
capability of piping device output to a program remains fully available through
`postscript(file = "", command = …)` and `<http://print.it|print.it> = TRUE`.

I have prepared a small patch to fix this problem which you can find at
<https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/pull/264> /
<https://patch-diff.githubusercontent.com/raw/r-devel/r-svn/pull/264.patch>
